constraints: An array BBB of length MMM is said to be dominating if each of its prefixes and suffixes has a majority element.
Formally,

For each iii (1≤i≤M1 \leq i \leq M1≤i≤M), in the prefix [B1,B2,…,Bi][B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_i][B1​,B2​,…,Bi​], there should exist an element that appears strictly more than ⌊i2⌋\left\lfloor\frac{i}{2}\right\rfloor⌊2i​⌋ times.

For each iii (1≤i≤M1 \leq i \leq M1≤i≤M), in the suffix [Bi,Bi+1,…,BM][B_i, B_{i+1}, \ldots, B_M][Bi​,Bi+1​,…,BM​], there should exist an element that appears strictly more than ⌊M−i+12⌋\left\lfloor \frac{M-i+1}{2} \right\rfloor⌊2M−i+1​⌋ times.
Here, ⌊x⌋\left\lfloor x \right\rfloor⌊x⌋ denotes the largest integer that doesn't exceed xxx.
For example, ⌊52⌋=2\left\lfloor \frac{5}{2} \right\rfloor = 2⌊25​⌋=2 and ⌊62⌋=3\left\lfloor \frac{6}{2} \right\rfloor = 3⌊26​⌋=3.
Note that it is allowed for different prefixes and suffixes of BBB to have different majorities - the only condition is that they must each have a majority.
You are given an array AAA of length NNN. Count the number of its contiguous subarrays that are dominating.
That is, count the number of pairs (L,R)(L, R)(L,R) such that 1≤L≤R≤N1 \leq L \leq R \leq N1≤L≤R≤N, and the subarray
[AL,AL+1,…,AR][A_L, A_{L+1}, \ldots, A_R][AL​,AL+1​,…,AR​] is dominating.
Input Format
The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of elements in the array.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — the elements of the array.
Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of dominating subarrays.
1≤Ai≤N1 \leq A_i \leq N1≤Ai​≤N
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

Explanation:
Test case 111: The dominating subarrays are: A[1,1],A[1,2],A[2,2],A[3,3],A[4,4],A[5,5]A[1, 1], A[1, 2], A[2, 2], A[3, 3], A[4, 4], A[5, 5]A[1,1],A[1,2],A[2,2],A[3,3],A[4,4],A[5,5].
Test case 222: Every subarray is dominating.
